speaker: interviewer
text: Told us that there were announcements [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], that if you would help someone who escaped a concentration camp, Stutthof for example, would be punished. Can you tell us more about that? And how much did you know about Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, as a boy, I heard that near of Gdansk, in German, Danzig, is a very hard concentration camp, Stutthof. Many Polish people when did something, or our Polish doctors, Polish professors, and priests, were sent to this concentration camp. Many times when happened something wrong, when from the underground they got, they caught them, the punishment was they sent to this concentration camp, Stutthof. We heard-- even in my house, I heard-- who went there, very certain camp, returned back. Through that, as I told you, in this shed, when I met the prisoners from this and were at Stutthof, my first impression was very anxious. I was-- I have seen on the display this-- some places, [SPEAKING GERMAN] there was warning, or warnung, warning. If you do, if you help to somebody, if you do this, you're punished. You're sent to the concentration camp in Stuttgart. Therefore, [? I had no ?] from Stuttgart, the prisoner. But Sigmund, his behavior was more, more serious. And he told me immediately, we must help them. We must do something. Therefore, two of them could stay a little longer until the darkness, because it was June, it was July, August. Rather, the night was not so dark. And he asked the two to stay. But the Pascal, he wanted to take him home. Therefore, we give him dresses. He could, could walk. Nobody could recognize that he is a prisoner when he has received [? civilian ?] dresses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know something about the prisoners? Were they Polish people? Were they Jews? Were they-- where did they come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean the three? Probably-- no, the two were Polish, because they spoke perfectly Polish. And one, he was, he told me he-- but he spoke a little German, few words only, that he's a Jew from France. And his name is Pascal. That I remember. And Sigmund took him in the, our dresses. Even me, it was so stupid, because I have to come back home without shirt, only with this white, you know, the striped undergarment. But I don't know exactly how with my mother they didn't see this. Maybe they didn't feel one shirt more or less. Anyhow, it was everything correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have told us that you sent the letter to Willi with your address on the letter. Did you get an answer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I haven't received any answer. And I was more anxious. I couldn't really sleep. My brother has seen my change. But I think he was happy that-- because, oh, I forget to say. One day, I stated him that I suffer very much, that I fall in love in him. He said, what? In soldiers, German soldiers, you fall in love? He hated-- I thought, he's not a German. He is other. Anyhow, but he dress the German uniform. And I [INAUDIBLE], don't worry. You like girls, and I like men. I like, I like him. Oh, if my mother, there's no-- but I never talk with mother about this. And it was [? lies. ?] And it was the last talking with my brother about this matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: I never forget the date. It was 19 September, 1942. I was working by [? Hanz ?] [? Krueger. ?] And one day, I was invited to the office to the chief. He said to me that I have to go to the Gestapo. I asked, what for? Go to the, to the Gestapo. And then usually, my mother has prepared some sandwiches, some [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know, some sandwiches with the bag. And I left this bag. He said, better you take this bag with you. What for? Take it with you. I didn't take, and I went to the Gestapo. I went to this Gestapo. They asked me first for the name. I told them my name. And they said, Heil Hitler. I couldn't reply Heil Hitler, because I was Polish. It was forbidden to mention Hitler. And they say, oh, you are this and this? Yeah. And he show me this letter, my own letter. I was what, my letter? This is your letter, yes? Yes. I couldn't lie. It was my letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have written this letter to German soldier. Yes, I did. OK. And then he took typewriter. He took a special application. And he wrote-- I never forget from this [? typewriter, ?] one part of this application. Says, it was written, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. When I saw this [SPEAKING GERMAN], it means that I will stay here. I am no more free. I will never go to my mother. I will never go home-- terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then, when he finish, you stay now here. What for? Don't play any theater. I started to cry. Don't cry. Don't play sadder. It will be everything [INAUDIBLE]. You will be free. Don't cry. Then somebody took me. And if you know the Gestapo uniform, took me to the cellar, a small cell. I never forget the furnish of the cell. There was only one wooden bed, and in the corner some-- some, for the, as a toilet. Next day morning, it was always-- how you say this? [SPEAKING GERMAN]. [SPEAKING GERMAN] is just like, they force it to know. They wanted to know so much as possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, where I met Willi, and then from this letter, they didn't know that there was something. No, I met on the street. And we talked. Nothing more. And then you write so lovely letter. You handsome. You [SPEAKING GERMAN]. No, it was only written.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet? Where did you? Tell us true. If you don't say true, you'll see what's happened. And if you tell us true, you will be sooner free, he promised me. I was always honest. I told him, I met him here. We have seen us. We discussed. I was playing [? this started. ?] Only this? Nothing more? Tell me true. You forget it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called two men. They took the belts with this [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And they beat me. They beat me as never before I was-- never. Nobody. My mother was very strong if I-- but never-- compare with this, nothing. I couldn't breathe. I couldn't speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: They put maybe two tubs of water, I don't know. And then again, you remember this? I have seen many [INAUDIBLE]. I have seen even soldiers order [INAUDIBLE]. And I never seen him. It was true. I never did. But they didn't believe me. And it was a trick of them. They said, this stated he has meet you. You, you gave him, you asked for this [? play ?] with this. He stated this. No. And really, I had no. I didn't-- never heard about such-- this view of love. So that-- you, you cannot remember? Once more.

speaker: interviewee
text: And maybe through two or three weeks, I don't know. I lost the calendar. I had no dates, no calendar. I don't know. Every day the same. [SPEAKING GERMAN] Every day the same. I was so tired. I was so ill. I prayed only that I will sign everything, even for my death. I will die, but I couldn't, I couldn't [? help ?] this more, this torture, torture. And then I remember, maybe after a [? month's ?] time, I came once more again. He gave, they gave me some, maybe two pieces of papers. And I had to sign. First of all, I signed with closed eyes. I don't know exactly what was there. I couldn't read so exactly German. I signed, because I thought just now I go to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. They gave me-- how you call this-- the hand shake. And they took me to a special prison car. It was closed. Where I'm going now? I will look a little in the car window. I have seen they took me to the, to the-- even before the war was in Torun, the prison, a stupid building, round building. Before we called it round building. How many pictures did they show you when they tortured you?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I cannot, but probably 50 or 60, maybe 100. Maybe one-- maybe one picture was known. He was one Polish guy, maybe. And later, I met him in prison. But I couldn't recognize. You know why? Because I was ill, because.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I was too anxious. I couldn't speak. I couldn't sleep. I couldn't eat. When I tried to sleep a little, [? there ?] only on my stomach. Because my back, it was [INAUDIBLE] with blood. There was not surface, nothing. It was-- so therefore, if I seen pictures, I couldn't recognize. I was so completely awake. They always, from time to time, they put water on me to make me fresh. But it was on the contrary. I was trembling. I was, it was cold, you know? But what did they want you to do to-- with these pictures, to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably they wanted to know. Maybe it's a crime. Or they had the prisoners. Maybe they were also, they were arrested. I don't know. I don't know the reason of them-- always on the tables, many pictures. But I never forget the trick. You, you liar-- he, he stated us he had with you. And now you say you had nothing? Once more-- maybe it was trick. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it Gestapo, or were there also SS officers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the black uniform, with the red here on this. It was Gestapo. This I know on the uniform. And that villa, it was occupied by Gestapo. Later, I knew that this villa belonged to one doctor, to medicine doctor. I don't know whether Jew or Pole. I don't know exactly. This place, after the war, I never seen. I have seen this prison on Torun, I cross. But I cross, you know, with closed eyes. I didn't wanted to see, because it was very touched for me to see the, the places.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were no SS officers during your--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Sometimes, there were also in civilian uniform. I don't know who was that. Men-- every day is a change, differently.

speaker: interviewer
text: But always the same people who tortured you?

speaker: interviewee
text: One or two of them, yeah. One or two to end. To the end of until I signature. But the other, they change always. And then, as I told you, when I signature, they gave me the hand sha-- and they took me by this car, by prison car, to the Pol-- to the prison, to [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: I never forget my first arriving there. They put me to this cell with eight or 10 prisoners. And one came. But first, they asked me immediately, the prison, what's the reason of my stay here. I said, oh, the stupid situation is. What kind of stupid? Oh, I wrote a letter. For letter writing, you are here in prison? Oh, you had maybe something. Maybe you are [INAUDIBLE]. And then said, yes, I wrote. Oh, you know what happened before the war with such like you? You could be here [INAUDIBLE]. We don't like such homosexual people. He mentioned in this way, pedo. They couldn't see the-- before the table. And I was really treated like second category of prison. I suffered very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to watch a little, to see where I am. I never forget when once, I'm watching through the window. The window was, of course, with this [? gate. ?] I saw my mother was standing. And she was, you know, she was watching hours long. She never saw me, but I have seen her. And she was standing. It was very touched for me, because I understand how she suffered. I was her lover's son. I was, I am looking like she exactly. And she was very proud for me. When we went on the street and some neighbors, oh, your son looks exactly-- yes, he's my son. And now, she is coming every day. And she was, she was watching and watching. It should be very, very hurtful for her. And so I was in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were mistreated by other prisoners. How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Due this reason that I stated, that I had something with Willi-- not exactly. But they called me-- they called me pedo. Pedo, it's mean like in German, [GERMAN], in bad way. And therefore, I was treated bad. If I were thief, if I murder, they could accept me, but not this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you beaten by other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, this, no. But if I wanted to have better bed place, no. You must go there. You, you have a smell. You have differently smell and this is, no. It was not nice. They, this, and without job-- only sitting, sitting. You can't imagine how long is the day. And then I was at the corner only sitting, so they never asked me for something without talking and talking. And I was, at this time, in my civilian clothes. And I was so hungry. Some of them, they dealed. For one piece of bread, they took one shirt, and they get me wrong, better in, in wrong equality. But I, I could give everything only to-- but even these small pieces of bread never filled me my-- my stomach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other prisoners who were called pedo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In my cell, no. And I didn't see-- in Torun, I didn't see, no. Later, yes, later. But in Torun, I didn't see. Our time was only morning for 20 or 15 minutes to run, to take a fresh air, to make the [INAUDIBLE] walk, walk, the walking. And then again, to the cell and sitting-- I don't know why without job, without nothing. And then, I'll never forget also this day. It was 5th December, 1944. The door was open, and the [GERMAN] called me. Come on, he said. He gave me also this. And he took me not in the street, only from this prison to the judgment that was-- later, I have seen it was a judge house. They had a special way, under way. Probably, this way is still now in Torun, because this judge house and this prison is still, is still standing. When they took me to this, to this judgment, to this judgment house, I looked. Behind me was mother. And there was this Heil Hitler, you know, these [INAUDIBLE], banner with [INAUDIBLE]. And five or six members of this in black dressing-- so not Gestapo, only just-- it's a judge, like judges. Yeah. First, they stated my whole-- how I was born, where I was living, when I this. And later, they started to shout. You must be punished. You must be isolated. You make demoralization with the German soldiers-- not only one, but all one army. How, you are like a whore, Polish whore. You must be punished. For five years, we punish you. And my mother was there. She was sitting. You can under-- I was [GERMAN], you know, [GERMAN]. I didn't kill nobody. I didn't steal never. And they punished me for five years. And after that, when they gave me [NON-ENGLISH] They gave me [NON-ENGLISH] [? since. ?] And I passed my mother. She smiled, only through a little. Me, too. We couldn't spoke, nothing. And it was the last seeing of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: The trial was in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Torun.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, the language was German they shouted at you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in German language. I understood not everything, but this, I remember their words, yeah. I told you only few sentences. But it was maybe--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they say in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe, maybe, maybe half, maybe one. But you see, I was standing. I was so-- and they talk and talk. I only kept some sentences from this. It was [? long, ?] the reading maybe like that. They put it on the head, you know, this terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say some of the things in German what they said to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know exactly the word, [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] is more heavy than prison. Young boys, 17-year boys, for [GERMAN]. You know [GERMAN]? Terrible. Where were you sent after the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately, the same day, in this, I was sent to Koronowo. Koronowo, it was before the war in Poland, also [GERMAN], only for long punishment, for murder and this, [? as it. ?] I never forget. I was, I was sent to a big room cells with about 40 prisoners. It was-- the smell was very bad. I have seen in there two or three-- this is in German-- [GERMAN], toilet [GERMAN], toilet [GERMAN]. Because you see, in the room, you have eaten, eaten. [INAUDIBLE] And there was piss, there was the toilet. If somebody had this diarrhea, he couldn't use it. Because in this cell, it was like mafia. The one boss, the one highest person and his clan, he had the rules of these cells. We had time for pissing, for using only morning, not evening, for taking the food, if we could. Because usually when the door was open, where the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] came. This is the name of the prisoners who brought the food. Let's say morning, they have small piece of bread, some margarine-- not every day, every second or Saturday-- and one spoon of marmalade, probably. Now I know, because this marmalade was so thin, like soup. Probably in the kitchen prison, steal something. [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH] also, they put water. The marmalade, it was so thin. But even this thin marmalade, I never tried. There, from mafia, the boss, he gave the top, and I have to give to this [NON-ENGLISH] put it. I give him. We were standing in the queue. And the next one, and everybody has to give the spoon of marmalade to, for him, so that he had. It happened the same with the margarine. And in this cell, when I arrived, I never forget. Maybe I can change-- I can mention now this name. Maybe this family is still alive. He was Apfelbaum. He was much older than me. He, he had a David star. He has seen that I was so young, shy.

speaker: interviewer
text: He asked me in Polish, what for? No, I told to him. It so stupid way. Maybe later I will explain. Because I was ashamed, because I was afraid that it will be happen the same situation like in the other prison, that they will treat me in the same way if I say so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, oh, don't worry, don't worry. But you are young. You'll stay alive. The war will be not forever. Oh, I like it very much that somebody said me in this way, especially when I was completely strange at these cells. Later, at the same, I met from Torun two gays. One is [? Janusz ?] M, and the other was [? Zbigniew ?] Z. Probably-- I know that [? Janusz ?] M Probably is no more alive, because when I went to Torun. I visited my parents, unfortunately, in the same old flat. Because even after the war, they never received again our nice flat. First took German, our flat, and then took Russian soldiers, the flat. And my poor parents, they had to stay to the end of their life in this very bad house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I visited them during the Christmas or all the holidays, I went also to th-- and I met once this [? Janusz ?] M. He was working in a house-- oh, [GERMAN]. But it was no more [GERMAN]. It was Polish government stock store, and he was working there. And once, I went also with my brother. I told him, come on, I will introduce [? Janusz, ?] when you would be in demand. Because in Poland, material was always very bad. Maybe he gives you something as we call [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. It means only as a connection for somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he used of this very often. He told me [? Janusz ?] was very kind. But later, when he went, he said, I never seen him. Last time, he was a little swollen. He was ill. Maybe he's dead. And the other one, he moved to other place. And I never seen him more.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were they? How old were they in the prison when you saw them?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old? How?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were [? Janusz ?] and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were a little older. [? Janusz ?] was older. [? Janusz ?] approximately so maybe 27. I don't know. I was the young. I was the youngest in the cell. Before, Applebaum, was very kind to me, like father, like grandfather. Because I think I was the youngest. And for-- probably he knew later. Because in the prison, in the cell, you know, it's going very, very quickly. And therefore, he wanted to show me so good behavior as it is possible. Of course, when, and then I received-- you know, there was two floor beds, down an upstairs. I was sleeping upstairs. Oh, there were many insects. I never forget, you know, the [GERMAN], in German. This-- terrible. And then there was the light coming through the cell, because in each corner, there was a special tower for watching if somebody tried to escape. Through that, I have seen. It was-- And usually at home, we prayed, as a Catholic, on the knee, on the, you know. But here, I couldn't, because I wanted to save the joking-- first of all, of the, as a gay, and then secondly, that I pray. But I prayed. I asked that I will be alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and so, one day-- I never forget this also-- once the [GERMAN] came to our cell and asked the boss, because I couldn't talk to the [GERMAN]. And he asked, do you know maybe some of the prisoners who knows German in writing and reading? Because we need for the-- as a writer. He gave somebody there from his of clan. But after few minutes, he came with him, and said, no, no good. No good. It's no good. [INAUDIBLE] many prisoners, 40 pris-- and you have nobody? I was thinking, maybe I can [? take. ?] I stood up. And I said, me, [GERMAN]. I speak German and write. You? You look so young. Yeah? Do you speak German? Yes, I do. Please, yeah, OK, you come with me. But you can be sure, if you don't speak and don't write in not good to write German, then you will know what happen, what will happen.
